excerpt: Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān defines faith as composed of three constitutive elements — inward belief, verbal declaration, and bodily action — none of which can be omitted without nullifying the whole, mirroring the tripartite formulation transmitted from Imam Jaʿfar al-Ṣādiq.
source_authority: Fatimid jurist, chief dāʿī under al-Muʿizz
source_location_hint: Vol. 1, Kitāb al-Walāya, opening discussion of īmān and its pillars

excerpt: Al-Sijistānī articulates islām as the ẓāhir (the manifest aspect) of religion and īmān as its bāṭin (the inner reality), so that the outward profession opens onto an interior assent that is the very meaning of the revealed law.
source_authority: Fatimid dāʿī and philosopher of the Iranian daʿwa
source_location_hint: Yanbūʿ on the relation between islām and īmān, ẓāhir and bāṭin

excerpt: the recognition (maʿrifa) of God and of the rightful imam is set forth as the first obligation, antecedent to every act of the limbs
source_authority: Fatimid chief qāḍī under al-Muʿizz
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Walāya, opening sections on the first obligation

excerpt: hearing as the threshold by which the soul receives the daʿwa
source_authority: Major Fatimid-Iranian dāʿī of the 4th/10th century
source_location_hint: Yanabīʿ on the order of the spiritual faculties

excerpt: the washing of the outward members corresponds to the purification of the inward faculties
source_authority: Fatimid Ḥujjat of Khurasan
source_location_hint: chapters on the taʾwīl of wuḍūʾ and ṣalāt

excerpt: the inner sense of ṣalāt as the soul's act of recognition and orientation toward the Imam
source_authority: Fatimid chief qāḍī under al-Muʿizz
source_location_hint: sections on the bāṭin of ritual prayer

excerpt: The believer's death is the soul's return to its origin: what the body fears, the refined soul greets with joy, because the veil of corporeality is at last withdrawn and the rightful hudud of the daʿwa are unveiled before it.
source_authority: Fatimid daʿi al-duʿat (d. 470/1078)
source_location_hint: Majālis on the soul's separation from the body and its meeting with the spiritual hudud

excerpt: The mustajib who has just entered the daʿwa stands to his dāʿī as a newborn stands to its parent; without the spiritual nurture of the higher hudud, the imperfect faith of the lower ranks cannot ripen into kamāl al-īmān.
source_authority: Fatimid daʿi of Khurasan (d. ca. 361/971)
source_location_hint: Sections on the hierarchy of the hudud al-din and the formation of the mustajib

excerpt: Whoever issues a ruling in religion by his own opinion, conjecture, or qiyas has set himself up as a partner with God in legislation; the believer takes his religion from the Imam, who alone preserves it from the corruption of innovation.
source_authority: Chief jurist of the Fatimid imams al-Manṣūr and al-Muʿizz (d. 363/974)
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Walaya, opening chapters on the obligation of returning every matter to the Imam

excerpt: The duties of iḥsān are graded: first parents, then kin, then orphans and the poor, then neighbours in descending order of bond, and finally wayfarers and dependants — each rank a step in the believer's training in mercy.
source_authority: Chief jurist of the Fatimid imams al-Manṣūr and al-Muʿizz (d. 363/974)
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Birr wa-l-Ṣila, on the rights of parents, kin, neighbours, and travellers

excerpt: The believer is one from whom only good is hoped and from whom no harm is feared; he counts his own good as little and his own evil as much, tires neither of serving people nor of seeking knowledge, prefers patience to pride and faqr to wealth, and reckons every god-fearing soul he meets as better than himself.
source_author: compiled by al-Sharif al-Radi from the sayings of Imam ʿAli ibn Abi Talib
source_authority: sermons, letters, and sayings of Amir al-Muʾminin ʿAli, compiled in the 4th/10th century
source_location_hint: Khuṭbat al-muttaqīn (the sermon to Hammām on the qualities of the god-fearing)

excerpt: Faith is the conjunction of inner conviction (taṣdīq bi-l-qalb), verbal profession (iqrār bi-l-lisān), and bodily action (ʿamal bi-l-arkān); deficiency in any one of the three lowers the believer's rank.
source_authority: Kitab al-Walaya, foundational treatment of the constituents of faith
source_location_hint: Volume 1, opening of Kitab al-Walaya

excerpt: ʿAlī's own recollection of being first to respond to the Prophet's summons and closest companion of his formative years, read by the Fatimid tradition as ontological proof of his walāya.
source_author: al-Sharif al-Radi (compiler); Imam ʿAli ibn Abi Talib (speaker)
source_authority: Khutba al-Qasiʿa (Sermon 192), on the priority and closeness of ʿAli to the Prophet
source_location_hint: Khutba al-Qasiʿa

excerpt: The temporal hierarchy of Muhajirun, Ansar, and Tabiʿin reflects the eternal hierarchy of the daʿwa; precedence in responding to the divine summons fixes both spiritual rank and place within the chain of duʿat.
source_authority: Majalis treating the gradation of the daʿwa and the metaphysics of precedence in faith
source_location_hint: various majalis on hierarchical structure of belief and the daʿwa

excerpt: The verbal shahāda places the believer within the protected community of Islam and binds him to the obligations of prayer, fasting, the alms-tax, the pilgrimage, and jihād — these being the outward pillars whose legal force flows from the act of confession.
source_authority: Fatimid daʿwa; teaching transmitted from the Imams of the ahl al-bayt
source_location_hint: Volume 1, Kitab al-Walaya and Kitab al-Tahara — foundational chapters on shahāda, the seven pillars, and the legal effects of the outward confession

excerpt: The First Intellect (al-ʿAql al-Awwal), originated by the command of the Mubdiʿ Taʿālā, confessed the unity of its Originator before any other act; only then was it endowed with the knowledge of all that was and shall be. Confession is therefore prior to knowledge in the spiritual order as in the human one.
source_authority: Fatimid daʿwa cosmology of the Iranian school
source_location_hint: The early yanbūʿs on the origination of the First Intellect (al-ʿAql al-Awwal) and its tawḥīd of the Originator

excerpt: Knowledge (ʿilm) and faith (īmān) are distinct yet interlocking: knowledge supplies the matter that faith affirms, while faith confers upon the soul the rank that mere knowledge cannot. The degrees of faith are erected upon the ladder of knowledge.
source_authority: Fatimid daʿwa; Persian Ismaili philosophical tradition
source_location_hint: Sections reconciling the prophetic and philosophical sciences and treating the relation of ʿilm to īmān

excerpt: Imam Jaʿfar al-Sadiq distinguished Islam from faith by saying that faith is wholly of the heart, while Islam is the observance of the rules by which life, property, marriage and inheritance are governed. Every believer is first a Muslim, but not every Muslim has yet become a believer.
source_authority: Imam Jaʿfar al-Sadiq, transmitted through the Fatimid daʿwa
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Iman within Daʿaʾim al-Islam — section on the difference between Islam and īmān

excerpt: Walāya is the seventh and most encompassing of the pillars; the obedience offered through the other six is accepted only through it.
source_authority: Fatimid chief jurist (d. 363/974)
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Walāya, opening discussion of the seven pillars

excerpt: The outward shahāda 'lā ilāha illā Allāh' conceals within it the inward shahāda 'lā ilāha illā huwa', whose pronoun refers to the Imām as the inward Lord of the East and the West.
source_authority: Fatimid-era Ismaili dāʿī (d. after 361/971)
source_location_hint: Yanbūʿ on the meanings of the shahāda

excerpt: The discharge of the outward religious obligations is the road by which the believer passes from the outer formula of unity to its inward truth.
source_authority: Fatimid-era Ismaili dāʿī of Khurāsān (d. ca. 481/1088)
source_location_hint: Discourse on the inner meaning of the kalima and the acts of worship

excerpt: The Muslim's final felicity (khātima bi-l-khayr) is not secured by outward obedience alone but by entering the covenant of walāya through which the Imām guarantees the believer's end.
source_authority: Fatimid chief dāʿī (d. 470/1078)
source_location_hint: Majālis on walāya and the sealing of faith

excerpt: The nāṭiq and the asās — the Prophet and the Imām — are the two adjacent ḥudūd of the higher daʿwa, and their mutual relation is the template by which every rank of the cosmic and earthly hierarchies is patterned.
source_authority: Ṭayyibī dāʿī muṭlaq in Yemen (d. 557/1162)
source_location_hint: Discussion of the ḥudūd of the daʿwa and the haykal
